data.epi_content:
"I want to have a meal together."

Honestly, I don't understand.

The dictionary definition of a meal is eating food at regular times like breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Or it refers to that food.

And generally, rather than having a meal with someone you dislike, if possible, people often prefer to go hungry or quickly eat alone.

Choking, indigestion, feeling uncomfortable... anyway, many unpleasant things happen.

Therefore, I never once expressed any desire to share a meal with this girl named Vivian.

Rationally, there was the unspoken hostility she would send my way if we stayed together, and emotionally, this young lady disliked it.

I wonder, is it that I dislike her, or am I just maintaining dislike out of habit?

Just like people who oppose for the sake of opposing, there are quite a few who hate for the sake of hating.

This young lady might be one of them.

Well, it's not important.

What's important is that for both me and this young lady, Vivian was a girl who became uncomfortable and dislikeable when together.

Even if cold tomato meatball spaghetti with overcooked noodles was on the menu that day.

So more precisely, rather than not understanding, I don't comprehend why she would say such a thing to me.

Why on earth would she want to have a meal with me? Is that what she's thinking?

"A meal?"

In a tone of rejection, I lower my voice and ask aggressively.

Then, showing she's not completely oblivious, Vivian slightly turned her eyes to the side and said quietly.

"Um... yes, a meal."

"And you want to eat with Evan, right?"

I spit out the words in a sharp, accusatory tone.

In a way, it felt like I was quietly reciting my emotions because I couldn't bring myself to get angry no matter how hard I tried.

At a glance, it might have seemed like just complaining.

But for this young lady, this was anger.

Just like walking with perfect posture even when ill because one must never lose dignity, her voice never rises.

If you were to question this young lady, she would answer as always that she was born this way and raised this way.

"This, this is just... not right. Don't you think?"

What a situation—the person someone likes comes to me saying they want to reconcile and become friends again, even though I dislike them.

Even a makjang drama wouldn't go this far. I haven't watched any, so I should probably add "perhaps."

Anyway, if it goes like this, I'll end up looking like someone who's stubbornly resisting the protagonist who's graciously trying to make peace despite having nothing.

A villain. Arrogant, self-centered, irritable, an unlikeable person no one wants to be around.

I wonder if Vivian is thinking about what she did wrong.

She's not answering hastily, rolling her eyes around as if searching for something to say.

Perhaps she's very likely to conclude that she didn't do anything wrong.

After all, she said she knew nothing of such green emotions as liking or loving.

That must be it.
data.epi_content2:
The protagonist in the novel might make small mistakes and face minor crises—crises that don't make people uncomfortable—but they weren't the type to tell lies.

So she can say she likes me—meaning "like" rather than "love."

If said in a sober state, it's either insanity, or she hasn't physically grasped the concept of love between the sexes, or it's a statement that changes the genre from romance to something else.

Even so, I never take my eyes off her.

Whether I've done something wrong or not, averting my gaze would be disrespectful and submissive.

I might make my expression a little more sorrowful when apologizing, though.

"Vivian, don't you think you're being a bit abnormal?

You must already know that I like, or liked, Evan.

If the person involved doesn't want reconciliation, it's not right to arrange it. I've told you repeatedly, we're not that kind of relationship."

"But before we fought, we were incredibly close!"

"Well, yes. I suppose we were."

I don't remember well.

It was probably a common or unimpressive event for this young lady.

Anyway, if Vivian says so, then that's how it was.

That's what she thought.

"So what? What's the same now as it was then?

Are there absolutely no differences between then—when I didn't envy or jealously you, and when Evan didn't say he liked you—and now?

If that's how you see it, fine, but I think a lot has changed."

At those words, Vivian starts to whimper slightly.

The first person to cry usually wins, but I decided not to think about that.

Come to think of it, just waking up here was already a defeat for me.

In a game with absolutely no chance of winning, at that.

"I don't think I've changed much since I first met you, Erica.

Back then, we would bicker and argue, like cute little chicks pecking at each other..."

"Back then, it was just that when childhood friends spend too much time together, they sometimes feel like siblings.

And if you're born as siblings, fighting with each other becomes your destiny, even if you don't do it in front of others.

It's just a joke, of course."

There's a certain emotional leeway that allows you to take whatever the other person says lightly.

Rather, siblings who don't have that space don't even talk to each other.

They just keep their doors firmly shut and always hope not to run into each other.

"I'm still the same,

Even though I've grown a little taller, I don't feel like everything has completely changed like other people say,

I still don't understand talk of becoming mature or growing up, so why do all of you change like that?

It's always felt like I'm the only one standing still in place."

Hearing this, I realized that although Vivian was speaking to me, she was also talking about other people she had met until now.

Well, with looks like hers, she probably hasn't lived a normal life.

Beautiful flowers always attract bees, butterflies, or flies.

"Why do you all always change your minds and come to dislike me?
data.epi_content3:
I just wanted to enter the Academy and make new female friends.

Why does everyone, from childhood, act like there's some predetermined agreement..."

"Vivian, you're excessively exceptional, and the people you've met so far have been excessively inadequate.

Think about the people you meet. A prince from the southern country, Evan who will inherit the duke's position, the Crown Prince, the Pope's hidden illegitimate..."

"What?"

She doesn't know yet.

"Let's move on. Anyway, the people you meet, those you consider friends, are all without exception people who are beyond being protagonists of the world—they could be called its masters.

You, merely a knight's daughter, mingle with them without any awkwardness. Already there, I won't use the term 'normal.'

Ordinary people would feel something strange. Or they would feel an overwhelming burden."

"Why on earth would there be any reason for that when we're just meeting as friends within the Academy?"

"Ordinary people find it impossible to even think that way.

It's just how it is. The pressure that comes with status is tremendous.

But you possess a beauty that surpasses status."

"Erica is beautiful too!"

"Thank you, but compared to you, I'm just ordinary."

"I don't... I don't understand...

If I'm hated by you, no, by others just because I was born pretty, how is that any different from being born ugly?

There couldn't possibly be fools who would torment someone out of jealousy for their beauty.

There couldn't be, my stepmother tormented me because I was unsightly and she couldn't stand to look at me..."

Her eyes look strange.

She's reciting the protagonist's trauma that I had only read about in a few lines of text.

At a glance, it seems like the ramblings of a madwoman.

But she's perfectly sane.

She's not the type to be frustrated by such things, and that level of discomfort hardly qualifies as trauma—it's just a slightly unpleasant childhood memory.

"There aren't many normal people; they're all humans with flaws.

And most have flaws in their appearance too."

And people always crave what they don't have,

As if entranced, thinking, "Could I ever have something like that?"

And since that's impossible for external appearances, most cases result in jealousy and hatred toward the other person.

"The God you all call omnipotent made humans that way, ha."

"I didn't choose to be born like this..."

She closes her mouth, perhaps realizing this doesn't sound right even to herself.

"Well, that's true, no one chooses how they're born.

Being born as a slave's child, spending your life toiling in sugarcane fields, gradually wasting away until death, or being born as a duke's daughter, living arrogantly and haughtily before suddenly falling from grace.

None of it was their own choice."
